content:
When the tool accelerates everything, the quality of whoever decides is the only differentiator left.

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The conversation about AI in 2026 has converged on one phrase that appears in every consulting report, every executive panel, every trend article: humans remain at the center of the decision. The phrase is technically correct, and what it leaves unsaid is almost everything that matters.
Because the center of the decision can be occupied by someone with the repertoire to evaluate what the tool produced, or by someone whose formal presence in the process changes nothing about the quality of what gets decided. Putting a human in the loop of an AI system does not guarantee judgment. It guarantees that someone signed off.
AI will not replace human judgment. It will make not having one more expensive.
There is a distinction the market is taking too long to name precisely, between using AI to execute and using AI to ground a decision. In the first case, the tool replaces repetitive effort and the gain is visible and relatively safe. In the second, it amplifies the analytical capacity of whoever already has clarity about what they are evaluating, and accelerates the mistake of whoever does not, with more confidence than they would have had without the tool.
A service business that uses an AI agent to map clients at risk of churn will receive a fast, organized list with patterns that would have taken weeks to identify manually. What happens with that list depends entirely on who reads it. If the leadership can distinguish clients with real margin from clients with apparent margin, knows which relationships have a viable substitute and which do not, and recognizes what the data did not capture because it was never recorded, the AI accelerated a good decision. If they cannot make those distinctions, it accelerated a bad one with an additional layer of technical authority that will make revision harder.
The MIT NANDA report (The GenAI Divide, 2025) identified a bottleneck that most executive panels are not naming: generative AI systems do not retain feedback, do not adapt to the operational context, and do not improve with use. Real return appeared where there was organizational structure to integrate the tool into existing workflows, not where there was enthusiasm for the technology. What that reveals, when examined carefully, is that the bottleneck is rarely the tool. It is what the organization had before turning it on.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The problem is not in the tool. It is in what comes before it.
Service businesses that arrive at AI without a clear operational diagnosis face a specific variation of this problem: the technology does not invent clarity about which business model still works, does not identify on its own which partner is the operational bottleneck no one has named, does not distinguish the strategic client from the client who occupies space in the portfolio simply because they have always been there. And it cannot perceive, because it has no way to perceive, that the process being automated is the wrong process.
This requires judgment prior to the tool. And judgment, in the sense that matters here, is not the intuition of an experienced partner, though experience helps. It is the capacity to distinguish signal from noise in a specific operation, with that operation’s data, accounting for what the data does not record. That is built through diagnosis, not through acceleration.
Three questions worth examining before scaling any AI initiative in a service operation. First: what exactly will the tool amplify? Without a precise answer to that, the risk is not falling behind. It is amplifying the existing problem at higher speed. Second: who will interpret what the AI produces, and does that person have enough clarity about the operation to challenge the recommendation when it is wrong? The quality of the decision is proportional to the quality of the repertoire of whoever interprets it. Third: was the operational diagnosis completed before automating, or is the automation a way of postponing that diagnosis? Automating an undiagnosed process is transferring a problem to a faster technological layer that is harder to correct.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The conversation about AI is right to say that the human remains at the center. It stays incomplete when it does not examine which human, with what level of clarity about their own operation, and capable of asking which questions before accepting any recommendation an agent produces in seconds. It is in this space, between the available tool and the judgment needed to use it well, that Pira Labs Consulting works with service businesses before the difference shows up in the result.
The tool became accessible to everyone at the same time. The capacity to judge what it produces still needs to be built before turning the machine on.
Whoever arrives at AI with diagnosis done accelerates what was already working. Whoever arrives without it discovers, faster than before, what they did not yet know about themselves.

Gabriela Aguiar and Celso Gama are the founders of Pira Labs Consulting, a Brazilian Creative Business Turnaround boutique for service businesses that need to turn weak signals into decisions, protect their reputation, and return to growing with margin, before the window of opportunity closes.
